title: Forces and motion — speed, velocity and acceleration
examTip: Always check whether a question asks for speed (scalar) or velocity (vector) — they're different quantities and mixing them up costs marks.
introTips: Speed and velocity look almost the same — the difference is direction, and that one detail matters enormously in physics.
Graphs are worth a lot of marks — know what gradient and area mean on both distance-time and velocity-time graphs.
Equations of motion questions nearly always give you three values and ask for a fourth — identify your u, v, a, s and t before writing anything.
intro: Every road sign, speed camera and sat-nav relies on the physics in this topic. Understanding speed, velocity and acceleration is how we describe motion precisely — and precisely is how you pick up every available mark in the exam.

keyPoints.heading: Scalar and vector quantities
keyPoints.content: Physics quantities are divided into two types. Scalar quantities have magnitude (size) only — there is no direction attached. Examples include distance, speed, mass, energy and temperature. Vector quantities have both magnitude AND direction — you must state both to fully describe them. Examples include displacement, velocity, acceleration, force and weight. Distance (m) is how far an object has moved along its path — it is scalar. Displacement (m) is how far an object is from its starting point and in which direction — it is vector. Example: you walk 300m east then 400m north. Distance = 700m. Displacement = 500m at an angle north-east (because you can calculate the straight-line distance from start to finish using Pythagoras). Speed (m/s) = distance ÷ time — scalar. Velocity (m/s) = displacement ÷ time — vector. The numerical value of speed and velocity can be the same, but velocity must always include a direction. 'A car drives at 20 m/s' gives speed. 'A car drives at 20 m/s due north' gives velocity.
writeBullets: Scalar — magnitude only: distance, speed, mass, energy, temperature
Vector — magnitude AND direction: displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, weight
Distance (m) — how far moved along the path (scalar)
Displacement (m) — straight-line distance from start with direction (vector)
Speed (m/s) — distance ÷ time, no direction needed
Velocity (m/s) — displacement ÷ time, direction must be stated

keyTerms.def: A quantity with magnitude only, no direction (e.g. speed, mass, distance)
A quantity with both magnitude and direction (e.g. velocity, force, displacement)
Straight-line distance from start to current position, including direction

keyPoints.memTip: SVD — Scalars: Speed, Volume, Distance. Vectors: Velocity, Weight, Displacement.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often write 'velocity = 20 m/s' without a direction and lose the mark — emphasise this every time.

keyPoints.heading: Speed, velocity and distance-time graphs
keyPoints.content: Average speed = total distance ÷ total time. Equation: v = d/t. Rearrangements: d = vt, t = d/v. Worked example: a cyclist travels 450m in 30s. Speed = 450 ÷ 30 = 15 m/s. Typical speeds to remember: walking ≈ 1.5 m/s, cycling ≈ 6 m/s, car on motorway ≈ 30 m/s, speed of sound in air ≈ 340 m/s, speed of light ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s. Distance-time (d-t) graphs: the gradient (slope) of a d-t graph equals the speed. A horizontal line means the object is stationary (distance not changing). A straight diagonal line means constant speed (distance increasing at a steady rate). A curved line means the speed is changing — if the curve is getting steeper, the object is speeding up; if it is flattening, it is slowing down. A steeper gradient on a d-t graph always means a faster speed.
writeBullets: Average speed (m/s) = total distance (m) ÷ total time (s), equation: v = d/t
d-t graph gradient = speed — steeper means faster
Horizontal line on d-t graph — object is stationary
Straight diagonal line — constant (steady) speed
Curved line on d-t graph — changing speed (acceleration or deceleration)
Typical speeds: walking 1.5 m/s, cycling 6 m/s, motorway car 30 m/s, sound 340 m/s

keyTerms.def: Total distance divided by total time for a journey
Slope of a graph — on a d-t graph, gradient equals speed

keyPoints.memTip: Speed = Distance over Time. Triangle: put D on top, v and t on the bottom.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Gradient calculations on d-t graphs come up almost every year — ensure Mabel can pick two points and calculate rise/run.

keyPoints.heading: Acceleration and velocity-time graphs
keyPoints.content: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Equation: a = (v − u) / t, where v = final velocity, u = initial velocity, t = time. Unit: m/s². Worked example: a car accelerates from 0 to 30 m/s in 10s. a = (30 − 0) ÷ 10 = 3 m/s². Deceleration is negative acceleration — velocity is decreasing. If u = 20 m/s, v = 0, t = 4s: a = (0 − 20) ÷ 4 = −5 m/s² (negative sign means decelerating). Velocity-time (v-t) graphs: the gradient of a v-t graph equals acceleration. The area under a v-t graph equals the distance travelled. A horizontal line on a v-t graph means constant velocity (zero acceleration). An upward slope means the object is accelerating. A downward slope means it is decelerating. A curved line on a v-t graph means the acceleration itself is changing (non-uniform acceleration). For a triangle on a v-t graph: area = ½ × base × height. For a trapezium: area = ½ × (sum of parallel sides) × height.
writeBullets: Acceleration (m/s²) = change in velocity ÷ time: a = (v − u) / t
Deceleration — negative acceleration, velocity is decreasing
v-t graph gradient = acceleration — upward slope means speeding up
Horizontal line on v-t graph — constant velocity, zero acceleration
Area under v-t graph = distance (displacement) travelled
Curved v-t line — non-uniform (changing) acceleration

keyTerms.def: Rate of change of velocity, measured in m/s²; can be positive (speeding up) or negative (slowing down)
Negative acceleration — the object is slowing down

keyPoints.memTip: v-t graph: Gradient = Acceleration, Area = Distance. GA-D: 'Gonna Ace D-grade no more!'
keyPoints.tutor_note: Area under v-t graph calculations appear as 6-mark questions — practise splitting shapes into rectangles and triangles.

keyPoints.heading: Equations of motion (SUVAT)
keyPoints.content: These equations link the five motion quantities: s (displacement, m), u (initial velocity, m/s), v (final velocity, m/s), a (acceleration, m/s²), t (time, s). You are given three values and asked to find a fourth. Equations: • v = u + at • s = ½(u + v)t • v² = u² + 2as (no time needed) Worked example 1: a ball rolls from rest (u = 0) with acceleration 2 m/s² for 5s. Find displacement. s = ½(u + v)t — but we need v first. v = 0 + 2 × 5 = 10 m/s. s = ½ × (0 + 10) × 5 = 25m. Or using v² = u² + 2as: 10² = 0 + 2 × 2 × s → 100 = 4s → s = 25m. ✓ Worked example 2: a car brakes from 20 m/s to rest over 40m. Find deceleration. v² = u² + 2as → 0 = 400 + 2a × 40 → 80a = −400 → a = −5 m/s² (decelerating at 5 m/s²).
writeBullets: s = displacement (m), u = initial velocity (m/s), v = final velocity (m/s), a = acceleration (m/s²), t = time (s)
v = u + at — use when you have u, a, t and want v
s = ½(u + v)t — use when you have u, v, t and want s
v² = u² + 2as — use when there is no time given
List known values before choosing which equation to use
Watch signs: deceleration gives a negative value for a

keyTerms.def: The five motion quantities: displacement, initial velocity, final velocity, acceleration, time

keyPoints.memTip: Write out s, u, v, a, t — tick off the three you know, circle the one you want, then pick the equation that contains all four.
keyPoints.tutor_note: v² = u² + 2as is the most tested because it hides time — make sure Mabel recognises when to use it.

keyPoints.heading: Terminal velocity and non-uniform acceleration
keyPoints.content: Uniform acceleration: the acceleration is constant — velocity changes by the same amount each second. Gives a straight line on a v-t graph. Non-uniform acceleration: the rate of change of velocity is itself changing — gives a curved line on a v-t graph. Free fall: ignoring air resistance, all objects near Earth's surface fall with the same acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward. Mass does not affect the rate of free fall. Terminal velocity: as an object falls, air resistance increases with speed. At some point, air resistance equals weight — resultant force = 0 → no further acceleration → the object falls at a constant (terminal) velocity. Skydiver story: (1) jumps — accelerates downward, air resistance small; (2) as speed increases, air resistance increases, net force decreases, acceleration decreases; (3) terminal velocity reached (~55 m/s) — constant speed; (4) parachute opens — drag increases massively, net force now upward, decelerates rapidly; (5) new lower terminal velocity (~5 m/s) reached — lands safely. On a v-t graph the skydiver's fall shows a curve that levels off at terminal velocity, then a sharp drop when the parachute opens, then levels off again at the lower terminal velocity.
writeBullets: Uniform acceleration — constant a, straight line on v-t graph
Non-uniform acceleration — changing a, curved line on v-t graph
Free fall — all objects accelerate at g ≈ 9.8 m/s² regardless of mass (ignoring air resistance)
Terminal velocity — air resistance equals weight, resultant force = 0, constant velocity
Skydiver accelerates → air resistance grows → acceleration decreases → terminal velocity
Parachute opens → massive drag → decelerates to new lower terminal velocity

keyTerms.def: Constant falling speed reached when air resistance exactly balances weight, giving zero resultant force
Falling under gravity alone with no air resistance; acceleration = g ≈ 9.8 m/s²

keyPoints.memTip: Terminal velocity: T for Terminal, T for Two forces balanced (weight = drag). No net force → no acceleration.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Describing the skydiver's v-t graph in words is a very common 4-mark question — practise the five stages.

tables.title: Distance-time vs velocity-time graphs — what each feature tells you

1.question: A car travels 240m in 8s at constant speed. What is its speed?
1.wrongFeedback: Use v = d/t. Speed = 240 ÷ 8 = 30 m/s. Don't add or subtract — it's a division.

2.question: On a velocity-time graph, what does the gradient of the line represent?
2.correctFeedback: Correct! Gradient on a v-t graph = acceleration (m/s²).
2.wrongFeedback: On a v-t graph, the gradient = acceleration. It's on a d-t graph that gradient = speed.

3.question: A motorcycle accelerates from 5 m/s to 25 m/s in 4s. What is its acceleration?
3.wrongFeedback: Use a = (v − u) / t = (25 − 5) / 4 = 5 m/s². Remember to subtract initial from final velocity first.

4.question: A ball is dropped from rest and falls freely under gravity (g = 9.8 m/s²) for 3s. How far does it fall?
4.correctFeedback: Correct! s = ½at² = ½ × 9.8 × 9 = 44.1m (using u = 0, so s = ½(u+v)t or v = at = 29.4, s = ½ × 29.4 × 3 = 44.1m).
4.wrongFeedback: Use v = u + at first: v = 0 + 9.8 × 3 = 29.4 m/s. Then s = ½(u+v)t = ½ × 29.4 × 3 = 44.1m.

commonMistakes: Writing velocity without a direction — always state the direction (e.g. '20 m/s northward') or you lose the mark.
Confusing gradient and area on v-t graphs — gradient = acceleration, area = distance. These are not interchangeable.
Using the wrong SUVAT equation — always list s, u, v, a, t and tick off the known values before choosing.
Thinking deceleration is a different thing from acceleration — it is just negative acceleration; use a negative sign in calculations.
Saying all falling objects fall at different rates — ignoring air resistance, all objects fall at the same g ≈ 9.8 m/s² regardless of mass.
examTips: In graph questions, always draw a large, accurate triangle when calculating gradient — use the full line, not just a small section.
For SUVAT questions, write the five letters s u v a t and tick what you know — it instantly shows which equation to use.
Terminal velocity questions ask for the shape of the v-t graph AND an explanation — describe the forces, not just the shape.
revisionCardBullets: Scalar — magnitude only (speed, distance, mass, energy)
Vector — magnitude + direction (velocity, displacement, acceleration, force, weight)
Speed v = d/t (m/s); velocity = speed with direction
d-t graph: gradient = speed; horizontal = stationary; curve = changing speed
Acceleration a = (v − u)/t, unit m/s²; deceleration is negative a
v-t graph: gradient = acceleration; area = distance; horizontal = constant velocity
SUVAT: v = u + at; s = ½(u+v)t; v² = u² + 2as
List s, u, v, a, t and tick known values before choosing equation
Free fall: all objects fall at g ≈ 9.8 m/s² ignoring air resistance
Terminal velocity: air resistance = weight → zero net force → constant velocity
Skydiver: accelerates → terminal velocity → parachute → decelerates → new terminal velocity
Typical speeds: walking 1.5, cycling 6, motorway car 30, sound 340 m/s, light 3×10⁸ m/s
